focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

Cora tied the mare to the rail and kept her palm against the animal's damp neck until the leather smell and the heat coming off the horse quit pushing at her face. After the talk in Hester's back room the night before, waiting another day had started to feel too much like helping August keep his story straight. In the yellow noon, the bank sat beyond the hitch rail like a loaf gone stale on the sill, its brick front filmed with dust where yesterday's wind had struck and dried. If she went in angry, August would let her spend it and call that a victory. She needed to go in hard instead. There was a difference.

She had carried the image for days now. Ezra sat at August's desk with the green blotter under his wrists. August's sleeve had been turned back once because of the heat, and the small white knife on the desk kept opening and shutting in August's fingers while Ezra bent his head and signed. Cora had tried, the first night after seeing it, to tell herself a dust-memory might lie in the way grief lied, by showing the worst shape first. Then Hester's jars had proved the opposite. The dust did not invent. It only refused mercy.

She crossed the sidewalk and went in.

Inside, the bank was cooler by three degrees and meaner for it. It smelled of paper, metal, old sweat, and dust trapped in cloth bindings. The wire of the teller cage cut everybody into squares. A ceiling fan turned with all the conviction of a tired spoon. Behind the wicket, Miss Lenz looked up from a stack of bills, rubbed her thumb and forefinger in the saucer of cornstarch she kept by the cash drawer so the notes would stop clinging in the heat, and gave Cora the cautious smile reserved for women whose names lived in ledgers.

"Mrs. Vale."

"I need Mr. Poeschel."

Miss Lenz glanced toward...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...hen back. "Ask for the note. The plat. The county-stamped copy. Ask why he needed your name before Knapp had seen the whole pile."

Cora thought of August's fingers flattening on the form, of the car by the pump, of Walter Knapp, decent enough to be unbearable, telling her there might still be time to choose what rode out on her own wagon. She thought of the word voluntary and felt her stomach turn.

"And if he won't?"

"Then he can refuse in words," Thea said. "That's worth more than another guess at this table."

"He can lie to my face then," Cora said. "I'll stand there and listen close."

Thea watched her for a long second. Then the corner of her mouth lifted. There was nothing funny in it. She recognized the choice and respected it despite herself.

"All right," she said. "Then when you go, go with the words themselves. Don't go in there with rage. Rage is what he expects from a debtor woman. Questions are harder on men like August."

"I can ask questions."

"You can ask them like nails."

"That too."

Dell, still baffled, said, "Somebody want to tell me what in hell either of you is talking about?"

Neither answered at once. Beans snapped. The kettle settled. Outside, the light went thinner over the yard and the wheat beyond it looked almost silver with dust.

Cora reached under the sugar jar and took August's note out from beneath it. She folded it once and slid it into the pocket of her apron. The paper warmed fast against her belly. It was barely any warmth, but it was enough.

"Eat first," she said. "Then we'll see what gets told."

Thea's satchel still leaned against the sideboard where she had dropped it, buckled tight over the attendance ledger and school papers she had not yet sorted. Cora left it there. One paper at a time was all the evening could stand.
